title: Psalms 137
subtitle: By the Rivers of Babylon

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — “How Can We Sing the Songs of the LORD in a Foreign Land?”

heb.paragraph: "By the rivers of Babylon (Bābel) we sat and wept when we remembered Zion. There on the poplars we hung our harps" (vv.1–2). The exile psalm. The captors demanded entertainment: "Sing us one of the songs of Zion!" (v.3). The response: "How can we sing the songs of the LORD while in a foreign land?" (v.4). The harps hung on trees: worship silenced by displacement. Music requires home.

notes.note: "By the rivers of Babylon, we sat and wept when we remembered Zion. We hung our harps on the poplars." The image: harps silent, tears falling, Zion remembered from exile. The most poignant scene of displacement in the OT.

"Our captors asked us for songs: ‘Sing us one of the songs of Zion!’ How can we sing the LORD’s song in a foreign land?" The impossibility: Zion’s songs require Zion. Worship cannot be performed on command for entertainment.

refs.note: The Babylonian exile separated talented youth from Jerusalem for royal service.
Jerusalem sits empty; Judah has gone into exile, dwelling among the nations.
'Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the Great!'—the oppressor's ultimate judgment.

hist.context: Psalm 137 is the exile's most poignant lament. 'By the rivers of Babylon we sat and wept when we remembered Zion.' The Babylonian captors demanded entertainment: 'Sing us one of the songs of Zion!' The exiles hung their harps on poplars, unable to sing the LORD's song in a foreign land. This psalm preserves the raw pain of displacement.

heb.paragraph: "If I forget you, Jerusalem, may my right hand (yəmînî) forget its skill. May my tongue cling to the roof of my mouth if I do not consider Jerusalem my highest joy" (vv.5–6). The oath of remembrance: may the musician’s hand forget how to play and the singer’s tongue stick — the instruments of worship destroyed — if Jerusalem is forgotten. Then the devastating imprecation (v.9): "Happy is the one who seizes your infants and dashes them against the rocks." The most disturbing verse in the Psalter. The raw cry of a people who watched their own children killed. Not a command but a grief-scream.

notes.note: "If I forget you, Jerusalem, may my right hand forget its skill." The self-imprecation: the musician curses his own hands and tongue if he forgets Zion. The instruments of worship become the instruments of the oath.

"Daughter Babylon, doomed to destruction... happy is the one who repays you according to what you have done to us." The imprecation is retributive: what you did to us, may someone do to you. The verse is horrifying because the original atrocity was horrifying. The psalm does not celebrate violence; it screams in the language of its own suffering.

refs.note: Edom is condemned for standing aloof during Jerusalem's destruction.
Isaiah prophesies Babylon's fall: 'Their infants will be dashed to pieces.'
Pay Babylon back double for what she has done—judgment matching crime.

hist.context: The psalm's closing verses are among Scripture's most troubling: blessing on whoever repays Babylon, 'who seizes your infants and dashes them against the rocks.' This invokes lex talionis—Babylon will receive what she inflicted (2 Kgs 8:12, Hos 13:16). The psalm gives voice to trauma's anguish while entrusting vengeance to God. Christian interpretation reads this as spiritual warfare against evil, not license for violence.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is not a key figure in Psalm 137. The psalm reflects the experience of exiles during the Babylonian captivity, which occurred long after David's death.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God is a key figure in By the Rivers of Babylon.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Solomon
ppl.text: Solomon is not a key figure in Psalm 137. The psalm reflects the Babylonian exile experience, which occurred centuries after Solomon's reign.

ppl.name: Abraham
ppl.text: Abraham is not a key figure in Psalm 137. The psalm focuses on the Babylonian exile experience and does not reference Abraham.

ppl.name: Jacob
ppl.text: Jacob is not explicitly mentioned as a key figure in Psalm 137. The psalm focuses on the exiles' experience in Babylon without specifically referencing Jacob.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVBy the rivers of Babylon we sat and wept when we remembered Zion.ESVBy the waters of Babylon, there we sat down and wept, when we remembered Zion.NIVRemember, Lord, what the Edomites did on the day Jerusalem fell. “Tear it down,” they cried, “tear it down to its foundations!”ESVRemember, O LORD, against the Edomites the day of Jerusalem, how they said, \u201cLay it bare, lay it bare, down to its foundations!\u201d

rows.label: If I Forget You — 137:5–9
rows.text: "If I FORGET you, Jerusalem." Right hand, tongue. The imprecation: the most disturbing verse in the Psalter.

chiasm.title: Psalm 137 — By the Rivers of Babylon

pairs.label: A
pairs.top: By the rivers of Babylon we sat and wept when we remembered Zion. There on the willows we hung our harps (vv.1–2)
pairs.bottom: Remember, LORD, what the Edomites did on the day Jerusalem fell. Daughter Babylon, doomed to destruction, happy is the one who repays you (vv.7–9)

pairs.label: B
pairs.top: Our captors asked us for songs, our tormentors demanded songs of joy: ‘Sing us one of the songs of Zion!’ (v.3)
pairs.bottom: If I forget you, Jerusalem, may my right hand forget its skill. May my tongue cling to the roof of my mouth if I do not remember you, if I do not consider Jerusalem my highest joy (vv.5–6)

center.label: X
center.text: How can we sing the songs of the LORD while in a foreign land? (v.4). The single question at the center defines the entire psalm — and the entire experience of exile. It is not a refusal to sing but a genuine theological crisis: can worship happen apart from the land, the temple, the presence of God? The answer the psalm gives is complex — we cannot sing for our captors, but we will never stop remembering.

hebtext.note: "By the rivers of Babylon (Bābel) we sat and wept when we remembered Zion. There on the poplars we hung our harps" (vv.1–2). The exile psalm. The captors demanded entertainment: "Sing us one of the songs of Zion!" (v.3). The response: "How can we sing the songs of the LORD while in a foreign land?" (v.4). The harps hung on trees: worship silenced by displacement. Music requires home.

"If I forget you, Jerusalem, may my right hand (yəmînî) forget its skill. May my tongue cling to the roof of my mouth if I do not consider Jerusalem my highest joy" (vv.5–6). The oath of remembrance: may the musician’s hand forget how to play and the singer’s tongue stick — the instruments of worship destroyed — if Jerusalem is forgotten. Then the devastating imprecation (v.9): "Happy is the one who seizes your infants and dashes them against the rocks." The most disturbing verse in the Psalter. The raw cry of a people who watched their own children killed. Not a command but a grief-scream.

themes.note: Judgment and Justice peak: the exile’s grief and the retributive imprecation. Covenant: the oath never to forget Jerusalem. The most emotionally raw psalm.

prayer_prompt: By the rivers of Babylon we sat and wept. Consider asking God about your exiles — the displacements that make worship hard and memory painful.
